quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between elastic and inelastic collisions?

options.text: Elastic collisions conserve both momentum and kinetic energy; inelastic collisions conserve only momentum
Elastic collisions conserve only energy; inelastic conserve only momentum
Inelastic collisions conserve kinetic energy; elastic collisions do not

quiz_questions.explanation: In elastic collisions, KE is conserved. In inelastic, some KE is lost (to heat, sound, deformation).

quiz_questions.question_text: Is momentum conserved in all collisions?

options.text: Yes, in an isolated system momentum is always conserved

quiz_questions.explanation: The law of conservation of momentum applies to all collisions when no external forces act.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a perfectly inelastic collision?

options.text: Objects stick together after collision (maximum kinetic energy lost)
Objects bounce off each other perfectly

quiz_questions.explanation: In a perfectly inelastic collision, objects merge and move together — maximum KE loss.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an elastic collision between equal masses where one is initially stationary, what happens?

options.text: The moving object stops and the stationary one moves at the original speed
Both objects continue in the same direction

quiz_questions.explanation: In a perfectly elastic collision between equal masses, momentum and KE transfer completely.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a collision, is kinetic energy always conserved?
quiz_questions.explanation: KE is conserved only in elastic collisions. Inelastic collisions lose KE to other forms of energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to the lost kinetic energy in an inelastic collision?

options.text: It converts to heat, sound, or deformation energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Energy conservation means the 'lost' KE transforms to other forms: internal energy, sound, etc.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the coefficient of restitution?

options.text: A ratio measuring the elasticity of a collision (0 = perfectly inelastic, 1 = perfectly elastic)
The ratio of final to initial mass
A measure of friction in a collision
The ratio of kinetic energy before and after

quiz_questions.explanation: e = relative velocity after / relative velocity before. e = 1 is perfectly elastic; e = 0 is perfectly inelastic.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can an object gain kinetic energy from a collision?

options.text: Yes, if another object does positive work on it
No, collisions only remove kinetic energy
Only if the objects have equal mass

quiz_questions.explanation: In elastic and some inelastic collisions, one object can gain KE from another.

quiz_questions.question_text: What distinguishes a 'super-elastic' (explosive) collision from an elastic collision?

options.text: Kinetic energy increases (e.g., from stored chemical or spring energy)

quiz_questions.explanation: Explosive collisions release stored energy, increasing total KE above the initial KE.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a two-body elastic collision, what two quantities are always conserved?
quiz_questions.explanation: In elastic collisions, both p = mv (momentum) and KE = ½mv² are conserved.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'impulse'?

options.text: The product of force and the time duration it acts (J = FΔt = Δp)
The energy stored in a collision
The speed gained after a collision
The work done in a collision

quiz_questions.explanation: Impulse equals the change in momentum: J = Δp = FΔt.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'conservation of momentum'?

options.text: The total momentum of an isolated system remains constant
Momentum is only conserved in elastic collisions

quiz_questions.explanation: Total momentum before = total momentum after, in the absence of external forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'deformation energy'?

options.text: Energy stored or released when a material changes shape during a collision

quiz_questions.explanation: Deformation energy is why inelastic collisions lose KE — it goes into bending, breaking, or squashing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'center of mass velocity'?

options.text: The velocity of the combined center of mass of a system (v_cm = total momentum / total mass)
The average of all individual velocities
The velocity of the heaviest object
The velocity at the center of collision

quiz_questions.explanation: v_cm = Σ(mv) / Σm; in a perfectly inelastic collision both objects move at this velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'rebound'?

options.text: When an object bounces back after collision with a surface or another object
When objects slow down and stop

quiz_questions.explanation: A rebound implies the object changes direction; in perfectly elastic rebound, speed is unchanged.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'collision in 2D'?

options.text: A collision where objects move in a plane and momentum is conserved in both x and y directions
A collision along a straight line only
A collision where only x-momentum is conserved
A collision where objects orbit each other

quiz_questions.explanation: In 2D, momentum conservation applies independently in both horizontal and vertical directions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'contact force'?

options.text: A force that acts only while objects are in physical contact
A force that acts at a distance
A force that is always constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Collision forces are contact forces — they only act during the brief moment of contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'total mechanical energy in collision'?

options.text: The sum of kinetic and potential energies of all objects involved

quiz_questions.explanation: Total mechanical energy before and after depends on whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'internal energy in a collision'?

options.text: Energy dissipated within the objects (vibration, heat, deformation)
Energy transferred to outside the system

quiz_questions.explanation: Inelastic collisions increase internal energy; this is where the 'lost' kinetic energy goes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg ball moving at 5 m/s collides with a 3 kg stationary ball. They stick together. What is their final velocity?
In the previous collision (Q20), how much kinetic energy was lost?
A 4 kg cart moving at 6 m/s collides elastically with a 4 kg stationary cart. What are the final velocities?

options.text: First cart stops; second moves at 6 m/s
First cart moves at 6 m/s; second stops

quiz_questions.explanation: Elastic collision between equal masses: the moving object stops and the stationary one moves at the original velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1 kg ball at 10 m/s collides with a 9 kg stationary ball. If perfectly inelastic, what is the final velocity?
In the previous collision (Q23), how much kinetic energy was lost?
Two equal masses move toward each other at 5 m/s each. If they collide perfectly inelastically, what is their final velocity?
quiz_questions.explanation: p_total = mv - mv = 0. Since v_f = p_total/(2m) = 0 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg object moving at 4 m/s collides elastically with a 15 kg stationary object. What is the velocity of the 5 kg object after the collision?
quiz_questions.explanation: Using elastic collision formulas: v_1f = (m_1 - m_2)/(m_1 + m_2) × v_1i = (5-15)/20 × 4 = -2 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 3 kg object at 8 m/s and a 2 kg object at 2 m/s move in the same direction and collide perfectly inelastically. What is their final velocity?
A proton (mass m) hits a stationary proton elastically. What fraction of the initial kinetic energy is transferred to the stationary proton?
quiz_questions.explanation: In an elastic collision between equal masses (like proton-proton), all KE transfers to the stationary one.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1200 kg car moving at 15 m/s rear-ends a 800 kg stationary car. They stick together. What is their combined velocity?
